results.title: Complaints & concerns | ProblemShared
results.content:
# Complaints and concerns

## When something goes wrong

We have the highest standards of care delivery at ProblemShared and really hope you never have cause to make a complaint. However, if you have been unhappy with your experience while using our service, you can find information about how you can raise your concerns and find a resolution below. [...] We've worked hard to create a supportive and transparent process for addressing any issues you may encounter. Please rest assured that every complaint is handled with care, respect, and a commitment to finding a resolution that leaves you feeling validated and supported.

Click here to file a complaint[]( [...] 4. The complainant must contact the Ombudsman’s office within 12 months of the incident causing the complaint. The complainant must explain what they are still not happy about, and why ‘Service Line Resolution’ did not work for them. They will advise the complainant, in writing, what they will do.

results.title: 10 Common Customer Complaints and How to Deal with Them l Square | Square
results.content:
How to handle it: Transparency is key to handling this issue. You need to speak with your supplier to understand when the next shipment will arrive, you need to pass this information onto your customer service and sales teams, and they must in turn pass it on to customers. Be proactive in your customer service, offering to call customers as soon as the next shipment comes in.

### 2. Broken/defective product

Complaint: When a product breaks or doesn’t perform as it should, you can expect to hear about it. This may not be your fault – it could be that the customer chose the wrong product or used it incorrectly. [...] Show understanding: To show you’ve been listening, acknowledge the issue that the customer has had by repeating it back to them. This isn’t about agreeing with the customer or accepting responsibility – it is simply a matter of showing that you understand their situation.

Ask questions: Once you’ve listened to and understood the complaint, it’s your turn to talk. Ask questions that get to the heart of the matter. Work to find out where the fault might lie. Always stay calm and kind. Avoid blaming the customer (even if the blame lies with them.) [...] How to handle it: If a customer needs to be passed from one team member to another, you need to ensure that the first person explains the situation to the second. Set up internal processes to make sure this happens, and apologise whenever a customer feels as though they’ve had to repeat themselves.

### 8. Issue not resolved on first call

Complaint: The customer made contact about an issue expecting it to be resolved then and there, but it wasn’t. Unless the problem is a particularly complex one, they have every right to be annoyed.

# 10 Most Common Customer Complaints and Their Solutions

No matter the industry, customer complaints are a reality of doing business. Complaints highlight areas where expectations aren’t being met, whether a delayed response, a billing error, or a frustrating return policy.

This is especially true in high-touch industries like banking, healthcare, and retail, where customer interactions are frequent and service quality matters most.

While complaints can feel like setbacks, they’re actually valuable insights. They reveal gaps in service, operational inefficiencies, and opportunities for improvement. [...] ## Why Listening to Customer Complaints Matters

Customer complaints are more than just frustrations. They provide valuable insights into service gaps, friction points, and unmet expectations. Yet, many businesses fail to recognize their importance.

Research shows that 96% of unhappy customers don’t complain, but 91% of them will leave without saying a word. This means businesses often lose customers without knowing why.

Frequent customer complaints act as early warning signals, helping businesses identify issues before they escalate.

When handled correctly, they can reduce churn, improve customer satisfaction, and strengthen brand loyalty. A well-resolved complaint can turn a frustrated customer into a long-term advocate. [...] ### 5. Repetitive Information Requests

Customers often find it frustrating to repeatedly provide the same personal information, account details, or case history. This is especially common in healthcare, banking, and customer support interactions, where inefficient data systems lead to unnecessary repetition.

To solve this issue, businesses can integrate self-check-in kiosks and CRM systems that allow customers to pre-fill or verify their details once, eliminating the need to repeat information.

For example, many healthcare facilities now use kiosks where patients can scan their IDs to check in rather than filling out paper forms multiple times. This speeds up the process, reduces frustration, and enhances the customer experience.

results.title: 15 Examples of Bad Customer Service & Ways to Fix Them - Velaro
results.content:
Damage to Brand Reputation: Dissatisfied customers are more likely to share their negative experiences, particularly when they feel there is a lack of customer service. They spread their frustration not just with friends and family, but on highly visible platforms such as social media or community forums. This amplifies the reach of their dissatisfaction, subjecting businesses to public scrutiny.

 Reduced Employee Motivation: Dealing with customer complaints without efficient customer service strategies can stress employees, causing burnout and reduced job satisfaction. This impacts motivation, leading to errors, absenteeism, and increased turnover, ultimately affecting the efficiency of customer service operations.

results.title: The complaint department never closes - by Suzan Bond
results.content: Tougher complaints are those that have turned chronic or habitual. Others will channel that unhappiness in your direction. Count on it. Sometimes people are just unhappy. They relish the opportunity to grumble. There’s probably not much you can do to make them content. Projecting our frustration outward is human nature—we do this when we feel scared, confused, or disempowered. As an authority figure, those feelings get sent in your direction. Sometimes it can turn into a way of loading frustration without taking responsibility for your actions. If not careful, leaders can take on issues that aren’t ours. The psychological term is projective identification. The hard part is that you can’t totally tune them out, sometimes they’re pointing to a common pain others have. [...] While I couldn’t resolve every complaint, I could manage my energy better. Here’s what I learned. Spend more time trying to understand why they’re complaining. This helps parse out the real complaints from habitual behavior. Sometimes they complain because they need to vent, hoping that externalizing their feelings will relieve stress. Or they grumble because they’re in pain. They know there isn’t resolution and just want it to be acknowledged. Offering a place for them to get it out can be helpful but make sure not to take it on personally. Other times complaining can also be a way of connecting with others, a form of bonding. This is ok. Unless it becomes overly negative or devolves into an us vs them situation with leadership, let it go. [...] Complaints aren't all negative. Raising concerns can be a way for people to give themselves a voice, can be empowering. And, there are real problems that need attention. Raising issues can elevate them to bring about change. Sometimes complaining about a decision to those in charge is exactly right. Humans are imperfect, so not every decision will be right—maybe not even many of them. Make sure you find and address real pain points causing interpersonal or organizational distress. It will build trust, empower the team and help maintain a positive culture.
